content.problemStatement:
Did you hear about Zeta Reticuli ? Its a star system deep in the southern sky, and this is where the first Alien film is set. Zetas are the aliens living there and they like playing games with humans. This year they selected Nancy and took her with them to play the famous Zeta-Nim game. Given N piles of stones and a value K, each pile i has a counter Ci which is the maximum number of stones that can be removed from it. Initially, Ci = K for 0 <= i < N. Each turn involves selecting one of the N piles, say pile i, and removing R ( 1 <= R <= Ci ) stones from it and updating Ci = R. In other words, the number of stones removed from a pile should not be more than the number of stones removed from the same pile before, starting with removal of at most K stones.


Zeta and Nancy take turns alternately and the one who can not remove any more stones lose. Given N, K and the number of stones in each of the N piles, find who wins the game if both play optimally and Nancy starts first.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and K, where N is the number of piles and K is the maximum number of stones that can be removed from each pile. The second line contains N space-separated integers representing the number of stones in each pile.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the name of the winner, either 'Nancy' or 'Zeta'.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The first test case has one pile with 3 stones and K = 1. Nancy can remove 1 stone, then Zeta can remove 1 stone, and finally Nancy can remove the last stone and win. The second test case has two piles with 3 stones each and K = 2. No matter what Nancy does, Zeta can always make the piles equal and win. The third test case has five piles with 5, 10, 15, 20, and 25 stones and K = 10. Nancy can remove stones from the piles to make the Nim sum non-zero and win.
Nancy can remove 3 stones from the first pile and 2 stones from the second pile, making the Nim sum non-zero and winning the game.
Nancy can remove the only stone from the pile and win the game.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using the concept of Nim Game. The key insight is to calculate the Nim sum of the piles after each move. The player who can make the Nim sum zero wins the game. We can calculate the Nim sum by XORing the number of stones in each pile after each move.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N) per test case, O(T * N) total. No precomputation is done.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — no extra space is used.
